Catalog Description

Special Topics in... Credit 1 to 4. Special topics in computer science that are new or

unique that are not covered in existing courses.

Additional Course Details

Course Description

Human Computer Interaction looks at the interactions between people and

technology. This course will help students understand cognitive and social aspects

to better understand, evaluate, and design the interface between technology and

humans. This course augments theoretical understanding with practical application.

Course Prerequisites

Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331 or concurrent enrollment; or approval of

instructor.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Critically evaluate  foundational theories and models of human-

computer interaction.

Construct  and  compare  cognitive and social user models.

Design  and  implement  strategies

for  collecting  and  interpreting  qualitative and quantitative user data,

and  draw  informed conclusions.

Synthesize  user requirements for effective interaction design

by  integrating  evidence from data analysis, user needs, and

contextual factors.

Apply  and  adapt  human-computer interaction principles to  design,

prototype,  and  implement  innovative interactive

systems,  justifying  design choices based on theoretical and empirical

evidence.

Formulate  and  execute  evaluations of interactive

systems,  critiquing  outcomes and  providing  actionable

recommendations for improvement.

Present  and  defend  findings and design decisions persuasively to

various audiences using appropriate formats.

Debate  and  critique  current practices and emerging trends in human-

computer interaction.

Special Course Designation

None

Study Abroad, Non-traditional delivery.

Grading Policy

Graded work modifications

If you want to challenge any grading, you must do so within one week of when the

grade is published.

We reserve the right to audit the grades for any assignments submitted to this

course. During the audit process, we can decrease or increase your score. This

could result in lowering the score of already released grades.

Grade Weighting

Pre-Class Engagement (7%)

Pre-class engagement can include but is not limited to course readings, quizzes,

polls, and surveys.

You get credit by submitting engagement submissions prior to the start of

class.

At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add

15% capped at 100%.

This allows you to miss some pre-class engagements without negatively

impacting your score. For example, a friend has a personal crisis, and

you choose to console them instead of doing some pre-class

engagements. You do not have to ask the instructor for special

consideration since it is built into the course.

Group Class Activities (8%)

You will work with people at your table or move to a table so that there are at least

3 people per group.

You will get credit if you attend class and you provide what is required for the

activity.

At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add

15% capped at 100%.

This allows you to miss some group class activities without negatively

Group Class Activities (8%)

You will work with people at your table or move to a table so that there are at least

3 people per group.

You will get credit if you attend class and you provide what is required for the

activity.

At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add

15% capped at 100%.

This allows you to miss some group class activities without negatively

impacting your score. For example, you had a flat tire on the way to

campus that caused you to miss a group class activity. You do not have

to ask the instructor for special consideration since it is built into the

course.

At least half of these activities expand HCI discussions to consider culture in

Singapore, the United States, and any other countries/cultures that students

can bring into the conversation.

Assignments (20%)

You will complete assignments throughout the semester either individually or with

others as specified in the assignment. Note that assignments done with others are

not a part of the group project.

Some assignments will be peer graded.

1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation

of other student assignments.

You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your

peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to

grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)

Current Research Presentation (10%)

You will present a summary of HCI research.

Your presentation will be based on one paper from an HCI conference or

journal.

Your presentation will be peer graded.

Grading

This will be peer graded.

Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation

of other student assignments.

You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your

peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to

grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)

Quick Applications (20%)

Timed assignments that have you do quick application of class materials. They are

designed to take 15 to 20 minutes while you will have at least 45 minutes to

complete them.

Team Project (30%)

You will work with a group on an in-depth HCI-based project on a topic selected by

your team with approval of the instructor.

Parts of this will be peer graded.

1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation

of other student assignments.

You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your

peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to

grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)

The instructor will establish a multiplier for you based on your team peer

evaluations.

1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation

of other student assignments.

You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your

peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to

grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)

The instructor will establish a multiplier for you based on your team peer

evaluations.

1. The instructor can modify the multiplier at their discretion.

For example, if you are not actively participating with your group

and you are assigned a multiplier of 0.9 and your group’s final

project score is 90, then your score would become 0.9*90 which

is 81.

Cultural Discourse (5%)

An important part of study abroad and HCI is learning about other cultures and

perspectives and how to interact appropriately with them.

You will receive credit for appropriately engaging with study abroad activities in

ways that embody the Aggie Core Values of Respect, Integrity, and Selfless Service.

This is in addition to any cultural discourse embedded in other graded work such as

assignments and in class activities.

Grading Scale

Grades will be rounded to the nearest whole number.

Grades will be assigned according to the following scale:

A:  ≥  90

B: < 90 and  ≥  80

C: < 80 and  ≥  70

D: < 70 and  ≥  60

F: < 60

These grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the  university's

grading system ):

A: excellent

B: good

C: satisfactory

D: passing

F: failing

If warranted, the course instructor may assign a failing grade regardless of a

student’s computed final score.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

General Guidelines

It is your responsibility to keep up with the class, even when unexpected

events interfere.

All requests for extensions due to excused absences must follow the process

for  documenting absences  below.

Penalties for unexcused late submissions are 5% per calendar day, unless

specified otherwise.

Each student/team has a limited number of "late days" they may use by

request as described below.

Submitting Late Work

1. Submit late work as usual.

Do not wait for approval of an excused absence since you might miss

the extended deadline while you wait.

2. Submit a late day request (after submission) via the appropriate link on the

course Canvas homepage.

3. Follow the process for  documenting absences .

Documentation accepted as excused will trigger the removal of any

penalty already assessed.

4. For team projects, late days are limited per team, not per individual.

the extended deadline while you wait.

course Canvas homepage.

3. Follow the process for  documenting absences .

Documentation accepted as excused will trigger the removal of any

penalty already assessed.

4. For team projects, late days are limited per team, not per individual.

Documenting Absences

Must be documented via the Absence Documentation link on the  course

Canvas homepage .

Do not send email. Instead put what you would have put in the email

into a simple document and submit that.

Note that documentation can be a rationale that you type into a document

and submit.

For example, you can say “I was sick”.

Further medical documentation will be needed if you are sick too

frequently.

Excused absences may receive an extension for graded work.

Note that the instructor has the discretion to approve an excused

absence when it does not fall within the automatic excused absences

defined by the university.

Teams are generally expected to complete work on time, even if a team

member has an excused absence.

Discuss team extensions for extenuating circumstances with the

instructor.

Unexcused Absences:

Standard late penalties apply.

Late days may be used up to the maximum outlined.

Extenuating Circumstances

For any questions or special circumstances, contact the instructor through Canvas,

the class chat, office hours, or make an appointment.

Course Schedule

There are at least 43 contact hours in this course:

36 = 4 hours per class day x 9 class days

5 = 5 hours at “hackathon”

2 = 0.5 hours per project milestone check in X 4 sessions

>0 = readings, assignments, & project

>0 = technical & cultural activities

Course Evaluation

Additional Course Information

Technology Requirements

Personal computer that fulfills the  Texas A&M Computer Requirements .

Used to consume content and interact with online course resources.

Able to connect your computer to project via an HDMI cable.

You will need an HDMI adapter if your device does not have an HDMI

port.

Wireless device to submit online forms during class.

Cell phones and laptop computers are common options.

Technology to Capture an Image

1. At times you may be asked to create a sketch or diagram to submit

electronically. You need the ability to get the diagram into a format that

can be uploaded. You could sketch on paper and take a picture with

your camera, sketch on a tablet device, or use a scanner. You should

be able to do this quickly, especially if needed for an online timed

submission.

Student Behavior & Academic Integrity

Acknowledgement

By submitting anything to this course, electronically or otherwise, you are asserting

the following: “On my honor, as an Aggie, I have neither given nor received

unauthorized aid on this academic work. In particular, I certify that I have listed

above all the sources that I consulted regarding this assignment and that I have not

received or given any assistance that is contrary to the letter or the spirit of the

collaboration guidelines for this assignment.”

Spirit & Intent

Assignments are designed to help you engage with HCI concepts. Doing anything to

subvert the learning objectives harm your learning experience and constitute

above all the sources that I consulted regarding this assignment and that I have not

received or given any assistance that is contrary to the letter or the spirit of the

collaboration guidelines for this assignment.”

Spirit & Intent

Assignments are designed to help you engage with HCI concepts. Doing anything to

subvert the learning objectives harm your learning experience and constitute

academic dishonesty.

Artificial Intelligence Statement

With the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, our creative processes are

evolving. However, these tools do not replace the essential skills of drafting, revising,

and reflecting on texts, programs, and lectures. Nor do they substitute for the

critical thinking required to locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite sources

responsibly.

Education is about preparing future innovators and leaders. That includes learning

to use emerging technologies ethically and effectively. In this course, you are

permitted to use generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Google Bard, DALL·E 2) to assist

with assignments if you choose to. These tools can help you brainstorm ideas or

provide feedback, but they should not complete your work for you.

To maintain academic integrity:

Cite any AI-generated content that informs your work. This includes in-text

citations, quotations, and entries in your reference list.

Failure to properly attribute AI-generated material is considered academic

dishonesty.

No more than 30% of any assignment should consist of AI-generated text.

For guidance on citing generative AI using APA 7th edition, refer to the APA Style

Blog for examples, including how to cite ChatGPT in both the body of your work and

your reference list.

Please be aware of the limitations of generative AI:

It often recycles existing ideas rather than producing original thought.

It may present false or misleading information alongside accurate content.

It can hallucinate—creating citations or facts that do not exist.

It may restructure your writing in ways that distort your intended meaning.

Always review and critically evaluate any AI-generated content before including it in

your work.

Recording statement

Students may not record audio or video of any course activity unless the student

has an approved accommodation from Disability Services permitting the recording

of lectures and/or laboratory sessions. This accommodation letter must be

presented to the instructor in advance of any recording being done. Students with

permission to record classes are not permitted to redistribute audio or video

recordings of statements or comments from the course to other individuals without

the express permission of the faculty member and of any students who are

of lectures and/or laboratory sessions. This accommodation letter must be

presented to the instructor in advance of any recording being done. Students with

permission to record classes are not permitted to redistribute audio or video

recordings of statements or comments from the course to other individuals without

the express permission of the faculty member and of any students who are

recorded.

Plagiarism and Copyright

All course materials are copyrighted and may not be copied or distributed without

permission. Plagiarism will be treated as a serious academic violation per university

policies.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

You are required to attend every class, arrive on time, and stay the whole time. You

are responsible for learning the material covered in class, regardless of your

attendance. As this is a study abroad program, your time in class is extremely

valuable. Each day of class abroad corresponds to approximately one and a half

your work.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

You are required to attend every class, arrive on time, and stay the whole time. You

are responsible for learning the material covered in class, regardless of your

attendance. As this is a study abroad program, your time in class is extremely

valuable. Each day of class abroad corresponds to approximately one and a half

weeks of class during a regular semester.
